---
title: "JSC370 Final Project Data Report"
format:
 html:
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  fig-width: 15
  fig-height: 10
jupyter: jsc370_final-venv
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd
from pandas.api.types import is_numeric_dtype, is_string_dtype
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
from sklearn.preprocessing import MultiLabelBinarizer
import matplotlib.cm as cm
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import seaborn as sns
import os
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score,mean_squared_error, r2_score, mean_absolute_error, f1_score, roc_auc_score
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, DecisionTreeRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, TimeSeriesSplit, train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from xgboost import XGBRegressor
```

```{python}
import warnings
from scipy.linalg import LinAlgWarning

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=LinAlgWarning)
```

```{python}
movies = pd.read_csv("data/processed/movies.csv")
casting = pd.read_csv("data/processed/casting.csv")
person = pd.read_csv("data/processed/person.csv")
person_movie_history = pd.read_csv("data/processed/person_movie_history.csv")
movies_enrich = pd.read_csv("data/processed/movies_enrich.csv")
```

#### Note:
- [View the GitHub repository](https://github.com/PatuanPurba/JSC370-Final_Project)
- [Download Project Report](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1pn6YbMe3m6HIub-1F68SeuzVb-ueOA1P?usp=sharing)

# Background
## Motivation
Movie analysis is a very interesting topic since not only can many factors be analysed, whether measurable directly or not. Some observable factors, such as production and distribution costs, theme and genre choice, and audience response factors, make the movie’s performance hard to predict. Further, there are also “invisible” factors that may affect a movie’s success, such as the plot storyline and the production team’s experience. These various factors make the movie’s performance often difficult to predict. 

This project focuses on observable features derived from movie metadata and team composition. These include movie-level characteristics such as genre, runtime, budget, and popularity, as well as team-related features such as cast size, crew size, and historical performance indicators of individuals involved in the movie (e.g., previous ratings and number of successful movies). By aggregating these features, we aim to approximate some of the “hidden” factors through measurable proxies.

In addition to cross-sectional relationships, movie characteristics and their association with performance may also change over time due to various reasons, such as the introduction of CGI and improvements in digital production, as well as shifts in distribution channels and global audience access. However, incorporating temporal effects directly into predictive models can be challenging, particularly when the nature of these shifts is unclear or difficult to encode through simple variables or interactions. For this reason, this project shifts the main reliance of temporal analysis from predictive analysis to data exploration and plotting

Despite the main analysis on graph analysis, this project still tries to encode “Big” events that supposedly change the underlying dataset structure by using a time indicator. Instead, temporal patterns are more explored through data visualization, examining how movie characteristics such as genre distribution, runtime, and team composition evolve between 2000 and 2025. Furthermore, plots are used to assess whether these shifts correspond to changes in movie performance. This approach allows for a clearer interpretation of temporal trends without introducing additional complexity into the modeling framework.

## Research Questions

- How strongly are movie characteristics and cast/crew statistics associated with movie performance 

- Is there any temporal shift in the movie’s characteristics, such as genre, length, and team size, between 2000 and 2025

- If there are any, how does the temporal shift affect the relationship between the movie’s information, including the team’s statistics, and the movie’s performance

# Methods
## Data Collection
3 types of datasets will be used in the analysis, with 2: movies and casts, which are directly fetched from the TMDB API. 

### Movies
The main dataset consists of movie information from movies that were released between 2000 and 2025, batched by release date. For each month of the release date, sorted by vote count, we try to fetch the first 20 pages for short_dataset, and first 40 pages for full_dataset, with each pages consist maximum of 20 movies. We assume that the more votes count, the more reliable the information we can get. 

### Cast and Crew
This dataset is an extension of the short_movies dataset, containing cast and crew for each movie ID. To reduce API calls, we use “append_to_response=credits” so each call on the second stage also includes the casts and crews. Since 1 movie can have several casts and crews, we split the dataset to allow testing analysis on a subset of the data. Due to a running time issue during row conversion, we only pick 10 casts and some “Key” crew from each movie. While the TMDB API already sorts the cast by character’s importance, this analysis may not be able to capture the effect of a popular cameo on the movie.

### Person
This dataset is a derivation of both datasets, Movies and Casting. First, we merge both datasets on Movie ID, then we group by pid to calculate the aggregate. This dataset serves as historical information of each person that are related to movies from the Movies dataset, including the Crew. Further, we may use this dataset to determine “hub” people and enrich the Movies dataset in further analysis.

## Data Wrangling
### Missing and Zero Values
During data exploration, one of the key pieces of information is 60% of “Budget” and “Revenue” observations are zero values (missing), which are the targets of our analysis. Due to logical reasons, we treat these zero values as missing values. Further, we leave zero values in Gender unchanged, but also change Male encoding from 2 to -1. Lastly, for the Popularity (Movies and Casting) and Length (min) columns, since only a few rows have a missing value, we will also simply convert those into missing values
Zero values substituted into missing values have some implications, such as making plotting and calculating quantiles easier, since np.nan values are usually excluded. However, it greatly reduces the number of valid observations for prediction analysis and may introduce selection bias, which turns the analysis back into zero values during prediction analysis.

### Feature Engineering
For this analysis, I added new features and financial metrics to enrich it. Firstly, we add a Bayesian “Weighted Rating” to handle movies with low rating counts, with “pseudo votes” of the 25th percentile of rating counts. Besides, I also added new financial metrics such as profit, Return on Investment, and yearly blockbuster indicator. As mentioned, this analysis also uses a time indicator of pre/post big event to try to encode the temporal shift. 
There are some other filters we use to ease the plotting and ensure the analysis is concise. We only use languages that have more than 200 observations, which leaves only 11 languages, and group the rest into “Others”. Second, to handle outliers on ROI, I will add an upper threshold based on the 99th percentile.
This analysis will also new dataset called Person_Movie_History to allow “Casts” impact during model building. This dataset will use Casting merged with Movies on Movie ID as the main foundation. Then, we will be grouped by pid chronologically, which means that for each row, we only use past rows to compute the aggregate. This dataset serves to minimize data leaking during model prediction. Further, this new dataset will also be used to produce the movie’s team statistics during feature engineering. 

## Statistical and Prediction Model
The main model would predict Rating and Revenue using only pre-release covariates. Analysis will avoid using the movie’s popularity, vote count, rating, revenue, and their derivative as predictors during model building, because the literature distinguishes between static pre-release factors and dynamic or post-release factors. In this project, those variables are better treated as outcomes, descriptive variables, or alternate targets rather than inputs for a pre-release prediction. However, we may use the past rows relative to the movie of those columns. We use these columns to build the movie’s participants (cast and crew) historical statistical data, and use it as predictors. Otherwise, only use post-release covariates during data exploration.
The prediction analysis will include 4 models, which will be tuned on selected hyperparameters with Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) as the evaluation metric. The first model is Ridge Regression, which assumes only a linear relationship and uses the hyperparameter alpha/penalty term. The second model is a Decision Tree with the hyperparameters being max depth and min sample per leaf. The third model is a Decision Tree with hyperparameters being the same as the previous model, plus the number of estimators and max features. The last model is XGBoost with the hyperparameters being max depth, learning rate, and regularization terms. 


```{python}
## Data Preparation

df = movies_enrich.copy()
df["Release Date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["Release Date"], errors="coerce")
df = df.sort_values("Release Date").reset_index(drop=True)
df["Quarter"] = df["Release Date"].dt.to_period("Q").dt.to_timestamp()
df["Budget (USD)"] = df["Budget (USD)"].replace(np.nan, 0)
df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(
    df,
    columns=["Language"],
    drop_first=False,
    dtype=int
)
df = df_encoded.copy()

df.drop(columns=["Release Date"], inplace=True)


features = [
    "Length (min)", "Budget (USD)", "Cast Size", "Crew Size",

    "has_cast", "top_cast_pop", "top_cast_career_span",
    "top5_cast_pop", "top5_cast_wavg_mrating", "top5_cast_avg_mpop", "top5_cast_nblockbuster", "top5_cast_nprofit", "top5_cast_career_span", "n_experienced_cast",

    "has_crew", "top5_crew_wavg_mrating", "top5_crew_avg_mpop", "top5_crew_nblockbuster", "top5_crew_nprofit", "n_experienced_crew",

    "has_director", "director_nmovies", "director_pop", "director_wavg_mrating", "director_avg_mpop", "director_recent_mpop", "director_nblockbuster", "director_nprofit", "director_career_span"
]

genre_features = movies_enrich.columns[movies_enrich.columns.str.startswith("genre_")].tolist()

Language_features = movies_enrich.columns[movies_enrich.columns.str.startswith("Language_")].tolist()

Time_features = movies_enrich.columns[movies_enrich.columns.str.startswith("Time_")].tolist()

features = features + genre_features + Language_features + Time_features

target_1 = "Rating"
target_2 = "Revenue (USD)"

df = df.dropna(subset=features + [target_2])
df["Revenue (USD)"] = np.log(df["Revenue (USD)"])

X = df[features]
y1 = df[target_1]   # or Rating / Revenue / Profit
y2 = df[target_2]

X_train, X_test, y1_train, y1_test, y2_train, y2_test = train_test_split(X, y1, y2, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, shuffle=False)
```




```{python}
## Summary Function Results

## Continous:
def verbose_results(results_df):
    summary = results_df[
    [
        "params",
        "mean_test_r2",
        "mean_test_neg_rmse",
        "mean_test_neg_mae",
        "rank_test_neg_rmse"
    ]].copy()

    summary["mean_test_rmse"] = -summary["mean_test_neg_rmse"]
    summary["mean_test_mae"] = -summary["mean_test_neg_mae"]

    summary = summary.sort_values("rank_test_neg_rmse")
    summary = summary.rename(columns={
        "params": "Parameters",
        "mean_test_rmse": "Mean RMSE",
        "mean_test_r2": "Mean R2",
        "mean_test_mae": "Mean MAE",
        "rank_test_neg_rmse": "Rank RMSE"
    })
    

    summary = summary[[
        "Parameters",
        "Mean R2",
        "Mean MAE",
        "Mean RMSE",
        "Rank RMSE"
    ]]

    display(summary.head())


def build_result_dict(results_df_1, results_df_2, model_name):
    best_1 = results_df_1.loc[results_df_1["rank_test_neg_rmse"] == 1].iloc[0]
    best_2 = results_df_2.loc[results_df_2["rank_test_neg_rmse"] == 1].iloc[0]

    return {
        "Best R2 (Rating)": best_1["mean_test_r2"],
        "Best MAE (Rating)": -best_1["mean_test_neg_mae"],
        "Best RMSE (Rating)": -best_1["mean_test_neg_rmse"],
        "Best R2 (log Revenue)": best_2["mean_test_r2"],
        "Best MAE (log Revenue)": -best_2["mean_test_neg_mae"],
        "Best RMSE (log Revenue)": -best_2["mean_test_neg_rmse"]
    }


```

```{python}
## Model Building
# List to store best model training statistics for each model
results_cv = []
```


```{python}
#| include: false
### Ridge Selection
# Ridge Regression with Grid Search

ridge_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=Ridge(),
    param_grid={"alpha": [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100]},
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    scoring={
        "r2": "r2",
        "neg_rmse": "neg_root_mean_squared_error",
        "neg_mae": "neg_mean_absolute_error"
    },
    refit="neg_rmse",
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

ridge_grid.fit(X_train, y1_train)
ridge_best_1 = ridge_grid.best_estimator_
ridge_results_1 = pd.DataFrame(ridge_grid.cv_results_)

print("Ridge Regression for Rating Prediction:")
verbose_results(ridge_results_1)


ridge_grid.fit(X_train, y2_train)
ridge_best_2 = ridge_grid.best_estimator_
ridge_results_2 = pd.DataFrame(ridge_grid.cv_results_)

print("Ridge Regression for Revenue Prediction:")
verbose_results(ridge_results_2)

results_cv.append(build_result_dict(ridge_results_1, ridge_results_2, "Ridge"))
```




```{python}
#| include: false
### Decision Tree Model Selection
# Decision Tree with Grid Search and Cost Complexity Pruning

param_grid = {
    "max_depth": [3, 5, 9],
    "min_samples_leaf": [1, 5, 20],
    "max_features": ["sqrt", "log2", 0.5, 1],
    "ccp_alpha": []
}

full_tree = DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=42)
path_r = full_tree.cost_complexity_pruning_path(X_train, y1_train)

ccp_alphas = path_r.ccp_alphas
ccp_alphas = ccp_alphas[:-1]

# Keep 0 as baseline: no pruning
ccp_alphas = ccp_alphas[ccp_alphas > 0]

log_alphas_r = np.logspace(
    np.log10(ccp_alphas[0]), 
    np.log10(ccp_alphas[-1]),
    num=6
)
# Make a Grid Search of Tree
param_grid["ccp_alpha"] = log_alphas_r

tree_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=42),
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    scoring={
        "r2": "r2",
        "neg_rmse": "neg_root_mean_squared_error",
        "neg_mae": "neg_mean_absolute_error"
    },
    refit="neg_rmse",
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

tree_grid.fit(X_train, y1_train)
tree_best_1 = tree_grid.best_estimator_
tree_results_1 = pd.DataFrame(tree_grid.cv_results_)
print("Decision Tree for Rating Prediction:")
verbose_results(tree_results_1)

# Log Revenue

full_tree = DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=42)
path_r = full_tree.cost_complexity_pruning_path(X_train, y2_train)

ccp_alphas = path_r.ccp_alphas
ccp_alphas = ccp_alphas[:-1]

# Keep 0 as baseline: no pruning
ccp_alphas = ccp_alphas[ccp_alphas > 0]

log_alphas_r = np.logspace(
    np.log10(ccp_alphas[0]), 
    np.log10(ccp_alphas[-1]),
    num=6
)
# Make a Grid Search of Tree
param_grid["ccp_alpha"] = log_alphas_r

tree_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=42),
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    scoring={
        "r2": "r2",
        "neg_rmse": "neg_root_mean_squared_error",
        "neg_mae": "neg_mean_absolute_error"
    },
    refit="neg_rmse",
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)


tree_grid.fit(X_train, y2_train)
tree_best_2 = tree_grid.best_estimator_
tree_results_2 = pd.DataFrame(tree_grid.cv_results_)
print("Decision Tree for Revenue Prediction:")
verbose_results(tree_results_2)

results_cv.append(build_result_dict(tree_results_1, tree_results_2, "Decision Tree"))
```




```{python}
#| include: false

# Random Forest with Grid Search
### Random Forest Model Selection
rf = RandomForestRegressor(random_state=42)

param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": [100, 200],
    "max_depth": [5, 10, None],
    "min_samples_leaf": [1, 5, 10],
    "max_features": ["sqrt", "log2"]
}

rf_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),
    scoring={
        "r2": "r2",
        "neg_rmse": "neg_root_mean_squared_error",
        "neg_mae": "neg_mean_absolute_error"
    },
    refit="neg_rmse",
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

rf_grid.fit(X_train, y1_train)
rf_results_1 = pd.DataFrame(rf_grid.cv_results_)
rf_best_1 = rf_grid.best_estimator_
print("Random Forest for Rating Prediction:")
verbose_results(rf_results_1)


rf_grid.fit(X_train, y2_train)
rf_best_2 = rf_grid.best_estimator_
rf_results_2 = pd.DataFrame(rf_grid.cv_results_)
# print("Random Forest for Revenue Prediction:")
verbose_results(rf_results_2)

results_cv.append(build_result_dict(rf_results_1, rf_results_2, "Random Forest"))
```




```{python}
#| include: false

### XGBoost Model Selection

param_dist = {
    'max_depth':     [4, 6, 8],
    'learning_rate': [0.05, 0.1, 0.5],
    'reg_lambda':    [0.1, 1, 10],
    'reg_alpha':     [0, 0.1, 1],
}

xgb_rs = XGBRegressor(
    n_estimators=100,  # hint: choose a fixed boosting-round budget for each sampled config
    random_state=42,
    eval_metric="rmse",
    n_jobs=-1
)

xgb_grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=xgb_rs,
    param_grid=param_dist,  # hint: number of random parameter samples to evaluate
    cv=TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5),  # hint: number of cross-validation folds
    scoring={
        "r2": "r2",
        "neg_rmse": "neg_root_mean_squared_error",
        "neg_mae": "neg_mean_absolute_error"
    },
    refit="neg_rmse",
    n_jobs=-1,
    verbose=1
)

xgb_grid.fit(X_train, y1_train)
xgb_best_1 = xgb_grid.best_estimator_
xgb_results_1 = pd.DataFrame(xgb_grid.cv_results_)
# print("XGBoost for Rating Prediction:")
# verbose_results(xgb_results_1)

xgb_grid.fit(X_train, y2_train)
xgb_best_2 = xgb_grid.best_estimator_
xgb_results_2 = pd.DataFrame(xgb_grid.cv_results_)
# print("XGBoost for Revenue Prediction:")
# verbose_results(xgb_results_2)

results_cv.append(build_result_dict(xgb_results_1, xgb_results_2, "XGBoost"))
```

# Results
## Prediction Analysis
### Model Comparison and Selection
```{python}
def sci_fmt(x):
    if pd.isna(x):
        return ""
    exponent = int(np.floor(np.log10(abs(x)))) if x != 0 else 0
    mantissa = x / (10 ** exponent)
    return f"{mantissa:.2f} × 10<sup>{exponent}</sup>"
```


```{python}
#| label: tbl-model-training-result
#| tbl-cap: "5-Fold Chronological CV performance of models for rating and log-revenue prediction"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

model_names = ["Ridge", "Decision Tree", "Random Forest", "XGBoost"]
results_df = pd.DataFrame(results_cv, index=model_names)
styled_result_table = (results_df.style
    .highlight_max(axis=0, subset=["Best R2 (Rating)", "Best R2 (log Revenue)"], color="yellow")
    .highlight_min(axis=0, subset=["Best MAE (Rating)", "Best RMSE (Rating)", "Best MAE (log Revenue)", "Best RMSE (log Revenue)"], color="yellow")
    .set_table_styles([
        {
            "selector": "caption",
            "props": [
                ("caption-side", "bottom"),
                ("text-align", "center"),
                ("font-size", "0.9em"),
                ("margin-top", "8px")
            ]
        }
    ])
).format({
    "Best R2 (Rating)": "{:.3f}",
    "Best MAE (Rating)": "{:.3f}",
    "Best RMSE (Rating)": "{:.3f}",
    "Best R2 (log Revenue)": "{:.3f}",
    "Best MAE (log Revenue)": "{:.3f}",
    "Best RMSE (log Revenue)": "{:.3f}",
})
styled_result_table

```

Table 1 compares the predictive performance of Ridge, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost models on rating and log revenue outcomes based on validation results during training. XGBoost achieved the best performance across all evaluation metrics, although the improvement over Random Forest was relatively modest, especially for rating prediction. Therefore, XGBoost was selected as the final model for feature importance analysis.

### Feature Importance Analysis

Getting the best model, now we want to try analyze deeper what are the strong covariates for each outcomes and compare whether it differs significantly. Further, we also want to see whether our encoding on Team Statistics can have any prediction power in the model. 

#### Rating
```{python}
#| label: fig-feature-importance-rating
#| fig-cap: "Feature Importance of Rating Prediction Model"
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false


model = xgb_best_1
X_used = X_train       
top_n = 10
plot_title = "Top Feature Importance for Movie Rating Prediction"

# ============================================================
# Helper: get feature names
# ============================================================

def get_feature_names(X_used, model):
    """
    Best case:
    - X_used is a pandas DataFrame, so columns are available.

    Uncertain case:
    - If you trained using NumPy array after preprocessing,
      the feature names may become f0, f1, f2, ...
    """
    if hasattr(X_used, "columns"):
        return np.array(X_used.columns)

    if hasattr(model, "feature_names_in_"):
        return np.array(model.feature_names_in_)

    # fallback if feature names are unavailable
    return np.array([f"feature_{i}" for i in range(model.n_features_in_)])


# ============================================================
# Helper: extract feature importance
# ============================================================

def extract_feature_importance(model, X_used, importance_type="gain"):
    """
    Works for:
    - RandomForestRegressor / RandomForestClassifier
    - XGBRegressor / XGBClassifier

    For XGBoost, default importance_type = "gain",
    which is usually more interpretable than "weight".
    """
    fitted_model = model

    booster = fitted_model.get_booster()
    importance_dict = booster.get_score(importance_type=importance_type)

    feature_names = X_used.columns

    importance_df = (
        pd.DataFrame({
            "feature": list(importance_dict.keys()),
            "importance": list(importance_dict.values())
        })
        .sort_values("importance", ascending=False)
        .reset_index(drop=True)
    )

    return importance_df


# ============================================================
# Extract importance
# ============================================================

importance_df = extract_feature_importance(
    model=model,
    X_used=X_used,
    importance_type="gain"   # XGBoost only; ignored for Random Forest
)

importance_top = importance_df.head(top_n).sort_values("importance")

importance_top["feature"] =  importance_top["feature"].replace({
    "top5_crew_wavg_mrating": "Top 5 Crew Weighted Avg Movie Rating",
    "n_experienced_cast": "Number of Experienced Cast Members",
    "top5_cast_wavg_mrating": "Top 5 Cast Weighted Avg Movie Rating",
    "director_wavg_mrating": "Director Weighted Avg Movie Rating",
    "top5_crew_avg_mpop": "Top 5 Crew Average Movie Popularity",

    "genre_Animation": "Genre Animation",
    "genre_Drama": "Genre Drama",
    "genre_Comedy": "Genre Comedy"
})


# PLOTTING

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5.5))

ax.barh(
    importance_top["feature"],
    importance_top["importance"]
)

ax.set_title(plot_title, fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Feature importance", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Feature", fontsize=11)

ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=10)

# Remove unnecessary borders
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

# Add light vertical grid
ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.25)
ax.set_axisbelow(True)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

```


#### Log Revenue 
```{python}
#| label: fig-feature-importance-log-revenue
#| fig-cap: "Feature Importance of Log Revenue Prediction Model"
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

model = xgb_best_2
X_used = X_train       
top_n = 10
plot_title = "Top Feature Importance for Movie Log Revenue Prediction" 

importance_df = extract_feature_importance(
    model=model,
    X_used=X_used,
    importance_type="gain"  
)


importance_top = importance_df.head(top_n).sort_values("importance")

importance_top["feature"] =  importance_top["feature"].replace({
    "top5_crew_wavg_mrating": "Top 5 Crew Weighted Avg Movie Rating",
    "top5_crew_nblockbuster": "Top 5 Crew No. Blockbuster Movies",
    "director_nprofit": "Director No. Profit Movies",

    "genre_Animation": "Genre Animation",
    "genre_Comedy": "Genre Comedy",
    "genre_Fantasy": "Genre Fantasy",
    "genre_Family": "Genre Family",
    "genre_Action": "Genre Action"
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5.5))

ax.barh(
    importance_top["feature"],
    importance_top["importance"]
)

ax.set_title(plot_title, fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Feature importance", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Feature", fontsize=11)

ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=10)

# Remove unnecessary borders
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

# Add light vertical grid
ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.25)
ax.set_axisbelow(True)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

```

Figure 1 and Figure 2 show that the strongest predictors differ substantially between audience rating and log revenue. Rating prediction is driven mainly by cast and crew quality-history variables, especially the weighted average rating of the top crew, while log revenue prediction is dominated by budget and genre. This indicates that audience evaluation appears more related to creative-team track record, whereas commercial performance is more strongly associated with production investment and genre marketability. 

### Ablation Test
```{python}
# Splitting All the predictors into 2 Categories, Metadata Information and the Movie's Team Statistics

metadata_cols = [
    "Length (min)",
    "Budget (USD)",

    "genre_Action",
    "genre_Adventure",
    "genre_Animation",
    "genre_Comedy",
    "genre_Crime",
    "genre_Documentary",
    "genre_Drama",
    "genre_Family",
    "genre_Fantasy",
    "genre_History",
    "genre_Horror",
    "genre_Music",
    "genre_Mystery",
    "genre_Romance",
    "genre_Science Fiction",
    "genre_TV Movie",
    "genre_Thriller",
    "genre_War",
    "genre_Western",

    "Time_Post Digital",
    "Time_Pre-Covid",
    "Time_Post Netflix Expansion",
]


team_cols = [
    "Cast Size",
    "Crew Size",

    "has_cast",
    "top_cast_pop",
    "top_cast_career_span",
    "top5_cast_wavg_mrating",
    "top5_cast_avg_mpop",
    "top5_cast_nblockbuster",
    "top5_cast_nprofit",
    "top5_cast_career_span",
    "n_experienced_cast",

    "has_crew",
    "top5_crew_avg_mpop",
    "top5_crew_nblockbuster",
    "top5_crew_nprofit",
    "n_experienced_crew",

    "has_director",
    "director_nmovies",
    "director_pop",
    "director_wavg_mrating",
    "director_avg_mpop",
    "director_recent_mpop",
    "director_nblockbuster",
    "director_nprofit",
    "director_career_span",
]
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-xgboost_ablation
#| tbl-cap: "CV performance of XGBoost for rating and log-revenue prediction by predictor category."
#| tbl-align: center

from sklearn.base import clone
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd


feature_sets = {
    "Metadata only": metadata_cols,
    "Team statistics only": team_cols,
    "Metadata + team statistics": metadata_cols + team_cols
}

results = []

for name, cols in feature_sets.items():
    model = clone(xgb_best_1)

    model.fit(X_train[cols], y1_train)
    y1_pred = model.predict(X_test[cols])

    model = clone(xgb_best_2)

    model.fit(X_train[cols], y2_train)
    y2_pred = model.predict(X_test[cols])

    results.append({
        "Feature Set": name,
        "R2 (Rating)": r2_score(y1_test, y1_pred),
        "MAE (Rating)": mean_absolute_error(y1_test, y1_pred),
        "RMSE (Rating)": np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y1_test, y1_pred)),

        "R2 (log Revenue)": r2_score(y2_test, y2_pred),
        "MAE (log Revenue)": mean_absolute_error(y2_test, y2_pred),
        "RMSE (log Revenue)": np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y2_test, y2_pred)),
    })

ablation_results = pd.DataFrame(results)
ablation_results

styled_ablation = (
    ablation_results.style
    .set_table_styles([
        {
            "selector": "caption",
            "props": [
                ("caption-side", "bottom"),
                ("text-align", "center"),
                ("font-size", "0.9em"),
                ("margin-top", "8px")
            ]
        }
    ])
    .format({
        "R2 (Rating)": "{:.3f}",
        "MAE (Rating)": "{:.3f}",
        "RMSE (Rating)": "{:.3f}",
        "R2 (log Revenue)": "{:.3f}",
        "MAE (log Revenue)": "{:.3f}",
        "RMSE (log Revenue)": "{:.3f}",
    })
)


# Highlight Team statistics only
styled_ablation = styled_ablation.set_properties(
    subset=pd.IndexSlice[1, ["MAE (Rating)"]],
    **{"background-color": "yellow"}
)

# Highlight Metadata only 
styled_ablation = styled_ablation.set_properties(
    subset=pd.IndexSlice[0, ["R2 (Rating)", "RMSE (Rating)", "R2 (log Revenue)", 
                            "MAE (log Revenue)", "RMSE (log Revenue)"]],
    **{"background-color": "yellow"}
)

styled_ablation

```

Table 2 compares XGBoost performance when the model is trained using only movie metadata, only team statistics, and both predictor groups combined. The combined model performs best across both rating and log-revenue prediction. This suggests that both movie-level information and cast/crew statistics contribute a useful predictive signal. However, the improvement is moderate, so the result should be interpreted as a predictive association rather than strong causal evidence. 

## The Relationship of Movie Characteristics and Cast/Crew Statistics With the Movie's Performancee
```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter-cast-stat-log-revenue
#| fig-cap: "Scatterplot of Top 5 Cast Weighted Average Past Movie's Rating vs Movie's Rating"
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| out-width: "100%"
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
temp_df = movies_enrich.copy()

# OPTIONAL: Randomly Sampling to make the graph less crowded
n = 3000
temp_df = temp_df.sample(n=n, random_state=42)


revenue_filter = (temp_df["Revenue (USD)"].notna()) & (temp_df["top5_cast_wavg_mrating"] > 0)
temp_df = temp_df[revenue_filter]

temp_df["Revenue (log USD)"] = np.log(temp_df["Revenue (USD)"])
temp_df["Budget (USD)"] = temp_df["Budget (USD)"].replace(np.nan, -1)


fig = px.scatter(temp_df, x='Rating', y='top5_cast_wavg_mrating',
                 color='Language', size='Revenue (log USD)', size_max=10,
                 title="Movie's Rating vs Top 5 Cast Average Past Movie's Rating",
                 labels={
                    "top5_cast_wavg_mrating": "Top 5 Cast Average Past Movie's Rating",
                    "top5_cast_avg_mpop": "Top 5 Cast Average Past Involvment Popularity",
                    "director_wavg_mrating": "Director Past Movie's Rating"
                 },
                 custom_data=['Rating', 'top5_cast_wavg_mrating', 'Revenue (USD)', 'Title', "Budget (USD)", "Release Date", "Cast Size", "Crew Size", "director_wavg_mrating", "top5_cast_avg_mpop"])

fig.update_traces(
    marker=dict(opacity=0.5),
    hovertemplate=(
        " Title: %{customdata[3]}<br>"
        " Release Date: %{customdata[5]|%Y-%m-%d}<br>"
        " Budget: $%{customdata[4]:,.0f}<br>"
        " Revenue: $%{customdata[2]:,.0f}<br>"
        " Director Past Movie Rating: %{customdata[8]:.1f}"
        "<extra></extra>"
    )
)

fig.update_layout(hovermode="closest")
fig.show()
```

Figure 3 suggests a moderate positive relationship between a movie’s rating and the average past rating of its top 5 cast members. Movies with higher-rated cast histories tend to receive higher ratings, although the points are still widely spread, meaning cast history alone does not fully explain movie rating. Most observations are concentrated around ratings of 5.5–7.2, with English-language movies dominating the sample. There is no obvious separation by language, so language may not be a relevant predictors for Movie's Rating.

## Temporal Shift in Movie's Characteristics and Performance

### Trend on Genre
```{python}
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 10
#| out-width: "100%"
#| label: fig-genre-counts-periods
#| fig-cap: "Movie counts by genre across release periods. Bar height represents the number of movies in each genre, while color represents the average weighted rating for that genre-period."
#| fig-align: center

genres = movies.loc[:, movies.columns.str.contains(r"^genre_")].copy()
genres = genres.rename(columns=lambda x: x.replace("genre_", ""))
genre_cols = genres.columns.tolist()

movies_with_genres = pd.concat(
    [movies[["Release Date", "Weighted Rating"]].copy(), genres],
    axis=1
)

movies_with_genres["Release Date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    movies_with_genres["Release Date"],
    errors="coerce"
)

# drop rows with missing release_date or rating
movies_with_genres = movies_with_genres.dropna(subset=["Release Date", "Weighted Rating"])

# Period Cut
periods = {
    "2000–2009": (2000, 2010),
    "2010–2019": (2010, 2020),
    "2020–2025": (2020, 2026)
}

# Fix global color mapping across all panels
all_genre_means = []
for start, end in periods.values():
    temp = movies_with_genres[
        (movies_with_genres["Release Date"].dt.year >= start) &
        (movies_with_genres["Release Date"].dt.year < end)
    ]
    for genre in genre_cols:
        mean_rating = temp.loc[temp[genre] == 1, "Weighted Rating"].mean()
        if pd.notna(mean_rating):
            all_genre_means.append(mean_rating)

# fallback if something goes wrong and list becomes empty
if len(all_genre_means) == 0:
    vmin, vmax = 0, 10
else:
    vmin = min(all_genre_means)
    vmax = max(all_genre_means)

norm = mcolors.Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
cmap = cm.viridis
sm = cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=cmap, norm=norm)
sm.set_array([])

# Plotting
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(9, 7), sharex=True, sharey=False)

for ax, (label, (start, end)) in zip(axes, periods.items()):
    temp = movies_with_genres[
        (movies_with_genres["Release Date"].dt.year >= start) &
        (movies_with_genres["Release Date"].dt.year < end)
    ]

    counts = []
    mean_ratings = []

    for genre in genre_cols:
        genre_subset = temp.loc[temp[genre] == 1, "Weighted Rating"]
        counts.append((temp[genre] == 1).sum())
        mean_ratings.append(genre_subset.mean())

    
    # Making the colors for plot
    mean_ratings_for_color = [x for x in mean_ratings]
    colors = cmap(norm(mean_ratings_for_color))

    ax.bar(genre_cols, counts, color=colors, edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.4)

    ax.set_title(f"Movies Released in {label}, by Genre", fontsize=12)
    ax.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.set_ylabel("Number of Movies")
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

# x-axis labels only once at the bottom
axes[-1].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
axes[-1].set_xlabel("Genre")



# single shared colorbar for all subplots
fig.subplots_adjust(right=1.1, hspace=0.25)
cbar = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=axes, orientation="vertical", pad=0.02)
cbar.set_label("Average weighted rating")

fig.suptitle("Movie Counts by Genre Across Release Periods", fontsize=15, y=0.98)
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0.93, 0.96])


plt.show()

```

From Figure 4, we can see that the composition of movie releases by genre is relatively the same across all decades, with drama, comedy, thriller, action, and horror dominating the genres. Note that the bottom graph only includes 5 years, explaining why the number of movies is only half than previous decade. Indicating that the number of movies released is relatively stable across years. 
We can see that there is a general increase in the average weighted rating for all genres, indicated by lighter colour in the later period. We can see that the most significant increase is in animation (from 6.5 to > 7). It may be explained by the recent quality and popularity increase in anime movies.



### Trend on Team Size and Movie's length
```{python}
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 10
#| out-width: "100%"
#| label: fig-team-size-length-periods
#| fig-cap: "Trend of Team Size (Cast and Crew) and Movie's Length Overtime."
#| fig-align: center
#| 
movies["Release Date"] = pd.to_datetime(movies["Release Date"])

temp_movies = movies_enrich.copy()

# We drop most recent quarter to ensure reliability
agg_date_movies = temp_movies.groupby("Year", as_index=True).agg(
    cast_size_median = ("Cast Size", "median"),
    crew_size_median = ("Crew Size", "median"),
    length_mean = ("Length (min)", "mean")
)[:-1]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(20, 5))

statistic_type = ["mean", "median"]
n_ticks = 6

ax = axes[0]

ax.plot(agg_date_movies.index, agg_date_movies["cast_size_median"], color="blue", label= "Cast Size (Median)", linewidth=0.8)
ax.plot(agg_date_movies.index, agg_date_movies["crew_size_median"], color="red", label= "Crew Size (Median)", linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Count")

ymin, ymax = ax.get_ylim()
ymin, ymax = np.floor(ymin), np.ceil(ymax)
ticks = np.linspace(ymin, ymax, n_ticks)
ax.set_yticks(ticks)
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)

# "Important Event"
event_years = {
    2010: "Digital Expansion",
    2019: "COVID-19",
    2023: "Post-COVID Recovery"
}
for year, label in event_years.items():
    ax.axvline(year, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    ax.text(
        year + 0.1,
        ymin + 0.7 * (ymax - ymin),
        label,
        rotation=90,
        va="top",
        ha="left",
        fontsize=8
)




# Movie's Length
ax_2 = axes[1]
series = agg_date_movies["length_mean"]

grand_mean = series.mean()
grand_std = series.std()

ax_2.axhspan(
    grand_mean - grand_std,
    grand_mean + grand_std,
    alpha=0.15,
    label="±1 SD",
    zorder=0
)

ax_2.axhline(
    grand_mean,
    linestyle="-",
    linewidth=1,
    alpha=0.7,
    label="Grand mean"
)

ax_2.plot(
    agg_date_movies.index,
    series,
    color="orange",
    label="Length (Min)",
    linewidth=0.8
)

ax_2.set_xlabel("Year")
ax_2.set_ylabel("Length (Min)")
ax_2.legend(fontsize=8)
ax_2.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)

ymin, ymax = ax_2.get_ylim()
ymin, ymax = np.floor(ymin), np.ceil(ymax)

# "Important Event"
event_years = {
    2010: "Digital Expansion",
    2019: "COVID-19",
    2023: "Post-COVID Recovery"
}
for year, label in event_years.items():
    ax_2.axvline(year, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    ax_2.text(
        year + 0.1,
        ymin + 0.7 * (ymax - ymin),
        label,
        rotation=90,
        va="top",
        ha="left",
        fontsize=8
)


lines1, labels1 = ax.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax.legend(lines1, labels1, loc="upper left")

fig.suptitle("Trends in Team Size and Movie Length Over Time", fontsize=13)
fig.subplots_adjust(wspace=0.25)

axes[0].text(
    0.5, -0.15,
    "(a) Median cast and crew size by year",
    transform=axes[0].transAxes,
    ha="center",
    va="top",
    fontsize=9
)

axes[1].text(
    0.5, -0.15,
    "(b) Average movie length by year",
    transform=axes[1].transAxes,
    ha="center",
    va="top",
    fontsize=9
)

plt.show()
```

These figures suggest that there are shifts in movie characteristics, but not all of them are significant. Median cast size stays relatively stable across 2000–2025, while the median crew size shows a clearer upward movement, especially after the mid-2010s and again in the early 2020s. This suggests that movie production may have become more crew-intensive over time.  In contrast, average movie length fluctuates around the long-run mean and mostly remains within the ±1 standard deviation band, suggesting no significant shift. Overall, these figures suggest there is little movement in the movie’s characteristics over time



# Conclusion
From the overall analysis, we found some insight that the movie’s metadata information and the Cast and Crew quality-history have some predictive power to predict the Movie’s performances. While they have quite similar performance on their own, their importance quite differs across the movie’s performance metrics. Team History Statistics variables are stronger on Rating prediction, while Metadata information, especially genre, is stronger in predicting Revenue. However, the predictive power is still so small that we can only take the relationship as predictive rather than causal. This suggests that while static historical team’s performance encoding has some predictive power, it is not enough to properly predict a movie’s future performance.

From the temporal analysis results and data exploration, we can also see that there is only a minor shift in the Movie’s characteristics. The relative proportion of movies per genre and the mean weighted rating is relatively the same across decades, except for Animation. Combining with how genre is an important predictor for Revenue, it may give a reason why most of the movies have a similar genre	

There are also some serious limitations during analysis that may make the result of this analysis less reliable. The first limitation is that many of the movies don’t report their budget or revenue, which leads to dropping many data points during model building. The second limitation is that we can’t use the Team’s statistics variables during temporal analysis since we built those with a specific start point. For example, many early movies may appear to have a less experienced Team since the dataset doesn’t record their past involvement. 

Overall, while this analysis gives some insight, the results of the analysis are quite disappointing. While our new encoding has a relationship with the movie’s performance, its power is not enough to predict rating or revenue. Besides, we also found only a little to no change in the shift of the movie’s characteristics, even during the “Major” event. Therefore, it’s recommended for future analysis to invent a better team performance and temporal effect encoding.
